justification: All 6 pass criteria met. (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) contain multi-paragraph substantive analysis with both positive and negative dimensions. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 8 distinct groups (B2C customers, B2B customers, support, ops/dispatch, drivers, API integrators, product/marketing, finance) each with specific impact descriptions. (3) 5 formal escalations raised for missing information (service tier scope, migration strategy, business motivation, B2B contracts, API compatibility). (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in BR-5, risk section, and a dedicated escalation. (5) confidence.level=52, well-calibrated and below 80, with clear assumptions listed. (6) Competitive positioning addressed in user and risk sections ('major carriers are increasing ETA precision, not decreasing it').

observations.text: Exceptional depth across all 4 business_impact dimensions — each includes both positive and negative framing, avoiding one-sided analysis
8 stakeholder groups identified (well above the 3 minimum), each with specific, actionable impact descriptions
5 formal escalations with structured options and recommendations — far exceeds the minimum of 1
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated — correctly reflects inability to assess contractual risk without contract information
Competitive risk explicitly called out: 'major carriers are increasing ETA precision, not decreasing it' — industry trend awareness
Identified non-obvious risks: API breaking changes for third-party integrators, regulatory risk for perishables/pharmaceuticals, timezone ambiguity
Business rules include invariant classifications with justifications — shows domain authority beyond surface-level analysis
Edge cases are practical and logistics-specific (timezone ambiguity, same-day delivery, multi-package orders, late-night orders)
In-flight order migration risk identified as an invariant (BR-3) — a critical operational concern many analyses would miss

Minor: the 'rejection_reasons' field is empty array — Bird could have noted conditions under which this proposal should be rejected outright (e.g., if B2B contracts are found with time-based SLAs and no amendment path)

trials.justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) contain substantive multi-sentence analysis. (2) stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact descriptions per group. (3) Three escalations for missing information (business motivation, SLA contracts, tier scope). (4) Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in both risk field and dedicated escalation. (5) confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. (6) Competitive positioning mentioned in financial impact, user impact, and risk sections.

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder breakdown with 7 groups including non-obvious ones like API partners, drivers, and product/sales team — far exceeds the 3-group minimum
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering business motivation, SLA audit, and tier scope — exceeds the 1-escalation minimum
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly reflects that without SLA contract data and business motivation, the analysis is directional but uncertain
Edge cases are excellent — same-day delivery incompatibility, timezone handling, B2B dock hours, and perishable goods show deep domain thinking
Business rules distinguish invariants from policy decisions with clear justification — BR-2 on SLA granularity is particularly strong
Balanced analysis throughout — operational benefit (reduced time pressure) is acknowledged but weighed against customer experience cost and metric gaming risk
Identifies the OTD metric gaming risk — date-only windows would artificially inflate on-time rates, masking real performance issues

trials.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are populated with substantive, multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeding the 3 minimum). Three formal escalations address missing information (service tier scope, business motivation, B2B contractual SLAs). Contractual SLA risk is explicitly identified in BR-4, AC-3, the risk sub-field, and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level is 45, well below the 80 threshold, with detailed assumptions listed. Competitive positioning is mentioned in both the financial and risk sub-fields.

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder breakdown with 7 groups, each with specific and differentiated impact statements — far beyond the minimum requirement
Three well-structured escalations with options and recommendations, covering service tier scope, business motivation, and B2B contractual risk
Confidence level of 45 is well-calibrated — correctly identifies that missing contract information and unclear scope make high confidence impossible
Financial analysis addresses both the mechanical KPI improvement (SLA penalty reduction) and the revenue risk from premium tier degradation
Operational analysis balances the benefit (reduced time pressure) against the cost (masking real performance problems) as the rubric requires
Edge cases are thorough and practical — same-day ambiguity, time zone handling, invisible intra-day delays, and dock scheduling are all real operational concerns
Competitive positioning explicitly noted: market moving toward more precision (Amazon, UberEats), making this a regression
Business rules distinguish invariants from non-invariants with clear justification, showing domain reasoning depth
The 10-25% SLA penalty reduction figure appears fabricated — no data was provided to support this estimate, and Bird should have flagged this as an assumption rather than stating it as a range
